title: GPU Deep Dive for ML
description: GPU architecture fundamentals — why GPUs are fast for ML, memory hierarchy, profiling model memory usage, mixed precision training, quantization, inference optimization (FlashAttention, PagedAttention, speculative decoding, continuous batching).
details: Modern ML training and inference is GPU-bound. Understanding GPU memory hierarchy (HBM vs SRAM vs registers) explains why FlashAttention is a breakthrough — SRAM is 100× faster than HBM but 1000× smaller. Mixed precision (FP16/BF16/FP8) and quantization (INT8/INT4) are essential for fitting large models on GPUs. Inference optimization has evolved rapidly: continuous batching (vLLM), PagedAttention, and speculative decoding each give 2-4× throughput improvements by eliminating the memory-bandwidth bottleneck of autoregressive decoding.

sections.title: Why GPUs Are Fast
sections.description:
[Intuition] CPU is a sports car (fast but limited parallel — 10s of lanes). GPU is a bus (slower per passenger but carries 100s at once). The GPU uses the SIMT model — 32 threads execute the same instruction on different data in a warp.

Key GPU concepts: Memory coalescing — adjacent threads access adjacent memory addresses, enabling a single wide memory transaction (64B per request). Latency hiding — while one warp waits for HBM (hundreds of cycles), the scheduler switches to another ready warp. Occupancy — ratio of active warps to maximum warps per SM. Higher occupancy = better latency hiding.

sections.title: GPU Memory Hierarchy
sections.description:
[Intuition] The 100× bandwidth gap between HBM and SRAM is the key insight behind FlashAttention. If you can keep data in SRAM instead of reading from HBM, you get a massive speedup.

[Concrete Numbers] H100 specs: 3.35 TB/s HBM bandwidth, ~50 TB/s SRAM bandwidth per SM (132 SMs × 228KB shared memory total). SRAM is ~15× faster per access, but the benefit is amplified because attention uses many passes over the data — FlashAttention reduces HBM reads from O(N²) to O(N² / M) where M is SRAM size.

sections.title: Memory Profiling for ML Models
sections.description:
[Practical Workflow] Before training any large model, calculate its memory footprint to determine if it fits on your GPU and what parallelism you need.

Formula breakdown: total_memory = weights + optimizer_states + activations (+ KV cache for inference)

[Concrete Numbers] Examples at BF16 (2 bytes per parameter):

ModelWeightsAdam StatesTotal TrainingGPU Config
LLaMA 3 8B16 GB64 GB80+ GBNeeds 1× H100 (80GB) with activation checkpointing
LLaMA 3 70B140 GB560 GB700+ GB7× H100s (FSDP ZeRO-3)
GPT-3 175B350 GB1.4 TB1.75 TBTP + PP + DP across dozens of GPUs
sections.codeBlock:
# Memory calculation for model training
# Model: LLaMA 3 8B, precision: BF16 (2 bytes)

n_params = 8e9
precision_bytes = 2  # BF16

# Weights
weights_mem = n_params * precision_bytes  # 16 GB

# Adam optimizer states: m (FP32) + v (FP32)
adam_mem = n_params * 4 * 2  # 64 GB (m and v in FP32)

# Activations (approx, varies by batch_size, seq_len, d_model)
batch_size = 1
seq_len = 4096
d_model = 4096
n_layers = 32
# Each layer stores: Q, K, V, attention output, FFN activations
act_mem = batch_size * seq_len * d_model * n_layers * precision_bytes * 34  # ~36 GB

# With activation checkpointing: don't store all activations
# Recompute during backward pass
# Trade: 15-25% extra compute vs 5-10x memory reduction
act_mem_checkpointed = act_mem / 6  # ~6 GB

# Total with checkpointing: 16 + 64 + 6 = 86 GB
# H100 has 80GB — need one more optimization (ZeRO-2 shards optimizer states)

# After ZeRO-2 (shard optimizer states across 2 GPUs):
# 16 + 32 + 6 = 54 GB per GPU

sections.title: Mixed Precision Training
sections.description:
[Intuition] BF16 is just FP32 with half the mantissa bits chopped off — same exponent range, less precision. You don't need loss scaling because the exponent has the same dynamic range as FP32. FP16 has fewer exponent bits, so it can underflow for small gradient values — hence the need for loss scaling.

Key patterns: FP16: range 2⁻²⁴ to 2¹⁵ (~6×10⁻⁸ to 65504), needs loss scaling. BF16: same range as FP32 (2⁻¹²⁶ to 2¹²⁷), no loss scaling needed. FP8: E4M3 for forward pass (more precision), E5M2 for backward pass (more range), new on H100. Master weights stored in FP32, forward/backward in lower precision.
sections.codeBlock:
import tensorflow as tf

# Enable mixed precision globally (BF16 on Ampere+ GPUs)
tf.keras.mixed_precision.set_global_policy('mixed_bfloat16')

# With mixed precision:
# - Forward/backward pass runs in BF16 (2x throughput, half memory)
# - Master weights in FP32 (preserve gradient precision)
# - No loss scaling needed (BF16 has same exponent range as FP32)

# Manual mixed precision loop for training
@tf.function
def train_step(x, y, model, optimizer):
    with tf.GradientTape() as tape:
        # Forward pass in BF16
        logits = model(x, training=True)
        # Loss is computed in FP32
        loss = tf.keras.losses.sparse_categorical_crossentropy(y, logits)
        loss = tf.cast(loss, tf.float32)
    # Gradients in BF16
    grads = tape.gradient(loss, model.trainable_variables)
    # Apply gradients with FP32 master weights
    optimizer.apply_gradients(zip(grads, model.trainable_variables))
    return loss

# For FP16 training on older GPUs, use loss scaling:
# optimizer = tf.keras.optimizers.experimental.SGD(1e-3)
# optimizer = tf.keras.mixed_precision.LossScaleOptimizer(optimizer)

sections.title: Quantization
sections.description:
[Concrete Numbers] LLaMA 2 70B at INT8: 70GB → 17.5GB (4× reduction). AWQ achieves this with 0.1% perplexity increase vs 0.5% for naive quantization.

Quantization compresses model weights from FP16/BF16 to lower precision (INT8, INT4), trading accuracy for memory and speed. Two main approaches: symmetric (just a scale factor, range is symmetric around zero) vs asymmetric (scale + zero point, can shift the range).

Calibration methods: absmax (scale = max(|w|) / 127 — simple but sensitive to outliers), percentile (use 99.9th percentile — handles the few outlier weights that would ruin absmax scaling).

Advanced methods: GPTQ (layer-wise quantization using approximate second-order Hessian information — optimal but slow, like a one-shot compression), AWQ (activation-aware — identify "salient" weights by measuring activation magnitudes, scale them up before quantization, then rescale after — preserves accuracy where it matters).
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np

def symmetric_quantize(weights, bits=8):
    """Symmetric INT8 quantization: scale = max(|w|) / (2^(bits-1) - 1)"""
    scale = np.max(np.abs(weights)) / (2**(bits-1) - 1)
    quantized = np.round(weights / scale).astype(np.int8)
    return quantized, scale

def asymmetric_quantize(weights, bits=8):
    """Asymmetric INT8: scale + zero_point allows shifting the range"""
    min_val, max_val = np.min(weights), np.max(weights)
    n_levels = 2**bits
    scale = (max_val - min_val) / (n_levels - 1)
    zero_point = np.round(-min_val / scale)
    quantized = np.round(weights / scale + zero_point).astype(np.uint8)
    return quantized, scale, zero_point

# Per-channel quantization: each output channel has its own scale
# This handles the key insight: different channels have different ranges
# Without per-channel: one outlier ruins precision for all channels

def quantize_per_channel(weight_matrix, bits=8):
    """weight_matrix shape: (out_channels, in_channels)"""
    scales = np.max(np.abs(weight_matrix), axis=1) / (2**(bits-1) - 1)
    quantized = np.round(weight_matrix / scales[:, np.newaxis]).astype(np.int8)
    return quantized, scales

sections.title: Inference Optimization
sections.description:
[Concrete Numbers] For a single H100 with LLaMA 2 7B: naive implementation = ~20 tok/s. With vLLM + PagedAttention = ~100 tok/s (5× improvement).

Key techniques:

Continuous batching (Sarathi/vLLM): Instead of waiting for all sequences in a batch to finish (static batching), dynamically add new requests as sequences complete. The GPU always runs at full utilization. This is the single biggest throughput win for LLM serving.

PagedAttention: The KV cache is fragmented in naive implementations — variable-length sequences leave unusable gaps. PagedAttention manages KV cache in fixed-size blocks (like OS virtual memory paging), eliminating fragmentation. Result: 2-4× throughput improvement just from better memory utilization.

Speculative decoding: A small "draft" model generates K tokens cheaply. The large target model then verifies all K tokens in a single forward pass (specifically, it computes logits for each position in parallel). If accepted (based on rejection sampling), we get K tokens for the cost of 1 target model pass + K cheap draft passes. Typical 2× speedup with no quality loss.

Token generation phases: Prefill (computes KV cache for the prompt — compute bound, ~1×) vs decode (autoregressive, 1 token at a time — memory bandwidth bound, ~20× more expensive per token).

[Practical Workflow] For production LLM serving: use vLLM or TensorRT-LLM with continuous batching and PagedAttention. Enable FP8 quantization for H100. If latency is critical, use speculative decoding with a 3-5× smaller draft model. Optimize prefill vs decode separately — prefill is compute-bound (use tensor parallelism), decode is memory-bandwidth bound (use more GPUs with FP8+quantization).

sections.title: FAANG Q&A — GPU Deep Dive
sections.description:
[Interview Angle] GPU questions test deep system understanding — the difference between compute-bound and memory-bound operations, and how modern optimization techniques (FlashAttention, PagedAttention) exploit the memory hierarchy.

  1. NVIDIA L5: "Why is inference memory-bandwidth bound while training is compute bound?" — Inference: for each token generated, we load the entire model weights (e.g., 16GB for 8B model) from HBM, compute one forward pass (maybe 10ms of compute), but spend 100ms on memory I/O. Training: process large batches (e.g., batch 64), amortize weight loading across many sequences, so compute dominates. The key metric is arithmetic intensity: FLOPs / bytes moved from memory.
  2. Google L5 / OpenAI L5: "How does FlashAttention reduce HBM reads?" — Standard attention reads Q, K, V from HBM (all O(N²) reads), computes S=QK^T, writes S to HBM, reads S, computes softmax, writes P, reads P, computes O=PV. Total: O(N²) HBM reads. FlashAttention: tiles the computation, loads blocks of Q/K/V into SRAM once, computes partial attention on the tile, updates the output in SRAM. Only the final result is written to HBM. HBM reads drop from O(N²) to O(N²/M) where M is SRAM size.
  3. NVIDIA L5: "Explain the difference between FP16 and BF16 — why does BF16 not need loss scaling?" — FP16 has 5 exponent bits (range 2⁻²⁴ to 2¹⁵), BF16 has 8 exponent bits (same as FP32, range 2⁻¹²⁶ to 2¹²⁷). Gradients during training are often tiny (2⁻¹⁰ to 2⁻²⁰) — FP16 underflows. Loss scaling multiplies the loss by a large constant (2⁸=256) before backward pass, shifting gradients into FP16's representable range, then divides at the end. BF16's wider exponent range covers tiny gradients without scaling.
  4. Meta L5: "How does continuous batching improve inference throughput?" — Static batching waits for all sequences in a batch to finish before starting new ones. Short sequences waste GPU time waiting for long ones. Continuous batching: after each step, check if any sequence finished; if so, evict it and start a new request immediately. The GPU always runs at near 100% utilization. vLLM's implementation achieves ~100 tok/s vs ~20 tok/s for naive.
  5. Google L5: "Your model needs 16GB of weights but the GPU has 40GB. Can you still train with batch size 64?" — Calculate total memory: weights (16GB) + optimizer states (64GB for Adam in FP32) + activations (batch 64, seq 4096, model 4096 → ~200GB without checkpointing). Answer: NO with naive settings. But with ZeRO-3 (shard optimizer + weights across GPUs), activation checkpointing, and gradient accumulation (simulate batch 64 with micro-batches of 4), yes. Tradeoff: more communication (all-gather + reduce-scatter) and recomputation overhead.

sections.title: References
sections.description:
• Dao et al. 2022 — FlashAttention: Fast and Memory-Efficient Exact Attention with IO-Awareness
  • Kwon et al. 2023 — Efficient Memory Management for Large Language Model Serving with PagedAttention (vLLM)
  • Frantar et al. 2022 — GPTQ: Accurate Post-Training Quantization for Generative Pre-trained Transformers
  • Lin et al. 2023 — AWQ: Activation-aware Weight Quantization for LLM Compression and Acceleration
  • Leviathan et al. 2022 — Fast Inference from Transformers via Speculative Decoding
  • Jouppi et al. 2017 — In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a Tensor Processing Unit (TPU architecture comparison)
  • NVIDIA H100 Tensor Core GPU Architecture white paper
  • NVIDIA CUDA Programming Guide (Memory Hierarchy, Warp Scheduling)
